aliases: Processed fish tofu with cheese filling

english: Processed fish tofu with cheese filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for cheese-filled fish paste tofu; closest match is fish paste product (FOODON_03315763). Cheese tofu is a processed fish paste product with cheese filling, commonly sold in Singapore.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fishery products — processed fish products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible match for processed fish paste product. No specific code exists for cheese-filled fish tofu.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT code identified for this processed fish paste food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cheese tofu is one piece weighing approximately 50g, commonly consumed as a snack or side dish at hawker centres and food courts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Tofu — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Tofu (F03040002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese tofu (processed fish paste with cheese filling). GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous processed fish cake / fish paste products with starch binders, referencing University of Sydney GI Database entries for fish-based processed products with low starch content. Glycemic load per 50g serving = (32 × 6.3 × 0.50) / 100 = 1.0, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 822mg per 100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese tofu; even a single 50g piece delivers approximately 411mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) and trans fat (~0.1g/100g) from processed cheese and deep-frying worsen insulin resistance despite low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although carbohydrates are low (6.3g/100g) and the GI is low (~32), the high saturated fat and trans fat content from processed cheese and deep-frying impair insulin sensitivity; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 50g or less and prefer baked or steamed preparations over deep-fried versions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (822mg/100g), estimated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g), and trans fat (~0.1g/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cheese tofu is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium content driving hypertensive risk, saturated fat contributing to LDL elevation, and the presence of trans fat from deep-frying; if consumed, limit to one small piece (≤50g) infrequently and choose steamed or baked preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat exceeds 5g/100g RA threshold; trans fat present; high sodium worsens tissue swelling; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cheese tofu is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to pro-inflammatory saturated fat, trans fat, high sodium, and AGEs generated by deep-frying; steamed or baked preparation reduces AGE burden but does not resolve the saturated fat and sodium concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese tofu is a popular Singaporean processed snack food consisting of a fish paste outer shell encasing a melted processed cheese filling. It is typically deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside, with a soft, savoury, and slightly cheesy interior. The product is classified as ultra-processed and belongs to the processed fish and seafood subgroup. It is widely sold at hawker centres, food courts, and convenience stores across Singapore, often consumed as a snack or side dish. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 207 kcal, 10.8g protein, 15.4g fat (including approximately 6.5g saturated fat and 0.1g trans fat from the cheese and frying process), 6.3g carbohydrates, 3.5g sugar, 1.2g dietary fibre, and a notably high 822mg sodium. The high sodium content is the primary clinical concern, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The saturated and trans fat content also raises concerns for Type 2 Diabetes management and cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), reflecting the low starch content of the fish paste base. Calcium content is 93mg/100g, contributed by the cheese filling. Cheese tofu is not suitable for vegans or those with fish or dairy allergies. It is generally considered halal in Singapore when produced by certified manufacturers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese tofu?
Is cheese tofu high in sodium?
What is cheese tofu made of?
Is cheese tofu suitable for hypertension?
How much protein in cheese tofu?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040002) with confidence 0.95. The food is a canonical Singapore processed fish product with an exact HPB record. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from the processed cheese and deep-frying preparation method as HPB does not separately report these sub-fractions; this is documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for similar processed fish paste products.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5g/100g) and trans fat (0.1g/100g) are estimated values derived from the known composition of processed cheese and deep-frying preparation, as the HPB Foodo database entry for F03040002 does not separately report saturated fat or trans fat sub-fractions. These estimates are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous processed fish cake products with cheese. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly equivalent processed cheese tofu product as of 2026. 3. GI value of 32 is an analogous estimate based on processed fish paste products with low starch content from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cheese tofu has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. The dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as cheese tofu contains fish paste and is not vegetarian or plant-based. 5. Halal status is conditionally true for HPB-listed products from certified Singapore manufacturers; consumers should verify certification on packaging. 6. All 10 aliases_by_language string values confirmed in flat aliases array.
